item.title: We’re Living in a Tick Nightmare. It’s Time to Go to War.
item.description: With ticks, we’ve barely begun to fight back. The longer we wait, the more territory they will claim across both our geographic and psychic maps.

item.title: The Democratic Establishment Had This Coming
item.description: The Democratic Party needs shaking up.

item.title: If the Democrats Actually Want to Win, This Is What They Have to Do
item.description: It is these issues that serve to reinforce the perception that Democrats are willing to sacrifice the interests of the working and middle classes.

item.title: The Ukraine-Russia War Reaches a New Phase
item.description: The ground war in Ukraine has stalemated and the crucial fight has shifted to the skies — missiles and interceptors.

item.title: The Housing Crisis Is Also a Retirement Crisis
item.description: A home has stopped being just a place to live — it has also become an asset.

item.title: Some Positive (and Negative) Thinking About Therapy
item.description: Readers respond to “Not Everyone Should Be in Therapy,” an Opinion guest essay by Harvey Lieberman.

item.title: The Ridiculous Rise of Victor Marx
item.description: Once started, the process of voter radicalization isn’t easy to modulate.

item.title: The Lo-Fi Way I Broke My Scrolling Habit
item.description: I tried many remedies to break my social media habit. A homemade hack worked best.

item.title: This Is One of the Loneliest Jobs in America
item.description: Times Opinion interviewed dozens of family caregivers across the country to reveal the emotional toll and personal sacrifices at the heart of America‘s elder care crisis.

item.title: Is Trump Following Viktor Orban’s Playbook?
item.description: Will Trump ultimately come to the same grief as Viktor Orban? On “Interesting Times,” columnist Ross Douthat asks writer Rod Dreher about the parallels between America and Hungary under Orban’s former regime.

item.title: What Americans Need to Understand About China
item.description: Kevin Rudd, a former prime minister of Australia, went back to school to try to understand how the Chinese president thinks.

item.title: Seneca’s Solution to the Crisis of Attention
item.description: Devote your attention to one idea a day.

item.title: What the Fight With Anthropic Reveals About Free Speech in America
item.description: We won’t get the A.I. regulation we deserve until we change how we think about the right to code.

item.title: I Teach at an Elite College and I Inflate Grades. Help Me.
item.description: Easy A’s are an easy out.

item.title: The Dangerous Appeal of Cruelty
item.description: When did politics get so cruel? On “The Opinions,” David French argues that we’ve started confusing cruelty with courage, rewarding politicians who break norms and mistake outrage for strength.

item.title: Are Fears of American Gerontocracy Fair?
item.description: Donald Trump. Mitch McConnell. American politics is once again grappling with the realities of aging leadership. On “The Opinions,” David Wallace-Wells argues that the United States has become a gerontocracy — and that as leaders stay in power longer, concerns about their health, transparency and ability to govern become harder to ignore.

item.title: Trump’s Corruption Stains, From Texas to Maine
item.description: Something is wrong if vice is all on their side and virtue is all on yours.

item.title: The Population Bust Is Coming Sooner Than Anyone Is Prepared For
item.description: America is not prepared for population decline.

item.title: The World Must Not Forget About the Genocide in Sudan
item.description: The longer the fighting drags on, the greater the chance that regional instability will spread.

item.title: The Tears, the Songs and the Memories
item.description: Readers respond to an Opinion guest essay by Roger Rosenblatt about why we cry. Also: Abortion prosecutions; Presidents Trump and Nixon.

item.title: Women Have Earned Our Menopause Revolution
item.description: A new generation is not willing to settle for being invisible.

item.title: ‘Platner Is Vile.’ McConnell Is Missing. Welcome to American Politics.
item.description: What America’s embarrassments have in common.

item.title: “The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire” Isn’t Saying What You Think It Is
item.description: In the same year as America’s founding, Edward Gibbon wrote “Decline and Fall.” 250 years later, it still holds lessons for us.

item.title: The Burdens and Benefits of Big Families
item.description: Readers respond to “Life Is Better With Siblings,” an Opinion guest essay by Catherine Ruth Pakaluk.

item.title: Fitness Influencers Are Not Making Men More Fit
item.description: Manosphere fitness influencers are the wrong model. Health requires looking at life as a whole, not as an exercise in “optimization.”

item.title: The World Is Cutting Ties With America. It’s Already Costing Us.
item.description: When other countries cut ties, Americans pay.

item.title: Serena Williams Is Back. Thank Goodness.
item.description: Until Serena Williams announced her return to tennis at 44, I never realized how much I needed her.

item.title: Jerrold Nadler: Penn Station Won’t Fund Itself
item.description: Penn Station needs massive fixes, but the Trump administration is going about it all wrong.

item.title: We Returned From China. We Realized Our Century’s Biggest Challenge.
item.description: A.I. demands a populist approach that treats the technology as a public project.

item.title: Looksmaxxers Actually Don’t Care What Women Think
item.description: Social pressure for men to look a certain way is coming primarily from other men.

item.title: In Sudan, a Preventable Atrocity
item.description: Hundreds of thousands of people in Sudan are bracing for their city to be overrun by a militia notorious for committing atrocities.

item.title: More Than One Way to Fix Social Security
item.description: Readers respond to two guest essays about how to cover the shortfall in the program’s funding before it’s too late.

item.title: ‘Platner Is Vile.’ McConnell Is Missing. Welcome to American Politics.
item.description: What America’s embarrassments have in common.

item.title: There’s Now No Place for Me in America, the Only Country I’ve Ever Known
item.description: Children of authorized visa holders in the United States have few means to remain after they age out of their parents’ work protections.

item.title: Euphemism Won’t Save Young Black Men’s Lives
item.description: The term ‘gun violence’ makes murder seem abstract, not a tragic daily occurrence.

item.title: The Very Good and Very Bad News on Climate
item.description: Advancements in renewable energy are paving the way for a new climate politics. The environmentalist Bill McKibben articulates some of the possibilities in this new era of energy abundance.

item.title: Why Democrats Are Tripping All Over Themselves
item.description: A longtime observer of the party tries to make sense of where the party is, and where it is going.

item.title: This Is a Lot More Worrying Than the Supreme Court’s Ruling on Executive Power
item.description: The White House is sidestepping Congress to make huge decisions for even the most important private industries.

item.title: Farewells to the Platner Campaign
item.description: Readers respond to an editorial about lessons from Graham Platner’s failed Senate campaign. Also: The Smithsonian’s goal.

item.title: Does Matt Damon Look Greek? No. So Stop Complaining About Lupita Nyong’o.
item.description: Helen of Troy was a legendary beauty, but also so much more.

item.title: ‘There’s No Graham Platner Without Donald Trump’: 3 Writers on the Fiasco in Maine
item.description: How much have the rules of politics changed?

item.title: The World’s Superpowers Are Scrambling for an Edge. It Makes All of Us Less Safe.
item.description: There’s a way for nations to avoid endless trade wars.

item.title: Are We Becoming ‘Weimar America’?
item.description: America is at risk of extreme political upheaval, the conservative writer Rod Dreher argues on “Interesting Times.” “It may not be Hitler 2.0, it may not be Stalin 2.0, it might be something all American, but it’s not going to be what we’re used to,” he says.

item.title: Are We Living Under Therapeutic Totalitarianism?
item.description: We’re in an age of “therapeutic totalitarianism,” the writer Rod Dreher argues, “where they give you everything you want, all the drugs, the porn, the sex, the entertainment.” All that’s required in return? “You just have to surrender your humanity,” he says on “Interesting Times.”

item.title: When Celebrities Protest, or Don’t
item.description: Readers, including the actor Debra Winger, respond to an essay by Molly Jong-Fast.

item.title: Why the U.S. Could Beat China
item.description: Chinamaxxing has Americans looking at China in a new light. But Dan Wang argues that despite China’s futuristic image, the United States still has key advantages.

item.title: Israel Is Not Invincible
item.description: There is suddenly daylight between the Trump administration and Netanyahu.

item.title: Five Questions That Will Determine America’s Next 50 Years
item.description: The truth worth celebrating this summer is that our country is still being made.

item.title: What We Should Learn From Nordic Happiness
item.description: Scandinavia is the land of five-week vacations, virtually free university, one-year parental leaves, easy child care and very happy citizens.

item.title: Founding Father vs. Foundering Toddler
item.description: No. 1 puts No. 45/47 to shame.

item.title: This Pathetic Groveling Is No Way to Rebuild a Party
item.description: The Democrats’ constant solicitations for cash do not make their supporters feel like they are on a winning team.

item.title: How to World Cup
item.description: New to soccer? You’re welcome here.

item.title: A.I. Is Strangling Our Economy
item.description: So much money is being poured into A.I. that the rest of the economy is starting to suffer.

item.title: The Transgender Sports Decision Was About Something Deeper Than Law
item.description: It’s never just a game.

item.title: Five Words That Shook the World
item.description: It wasn’t Thomas Jefferson who made “All men are created equal” actually mean something.

item.title: Parents Can’t Give Their Kids Everything. Siblings Can Help.
item.description: There are virtues in growing up in a pack.

item.title: The Trump Vibe Shift Was Just a Mirage
item.description: The country does not love its president.

item.title: Elon Musk Is Too Rich. We’ve Known That Since Plato.
item.description: The philosopher made a radical proposal about wealth.

item.title: Creature From the Green Lagoon
item.description: Trump’s slimy, stinky swamp within the swamp.

item.title: My Father’s Death Was the Start of My Life
item.description: It was the worst thing that ever happened to me. It also taught me everything I needed to live.

item.title: Trump Was Right. The War Ended With a Surrender.
item.description: Each generation seems destined to learn anew the lesson that when hawks declare that a war will be painless, beware.

item.title: We Need to Keep Funding Weird Science
item.description: Science turned a serendipitous finding about lizard venom into one of the most important drugs of the century, but that type of research is getting harder to do.

item.title: In Britain, the Far Right Is Moving to the Mainstream
item.description: Its opponents urgently need to demonstrate that they can do more than throw up their hands in horror.

item.title: We Ran the Numbers. Remote Work Is Bad for Us.
item.description: Despite its advantages, work-from-home arrangements have significantly deepened Americans’ isolation and distress.

item.title: Why the Knicks Really Won
item.description: Teamwork isn’t just useful, the Knicks showed. It’s a higher calling.

item.title: JD Vance on the Morality of the Trump Administration
item.description: I asked the vice president what is Christian about this White House.
